items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.correct: Visual equilibrium achieved with different elements of equal visual weight on each side.
items.why: Students confuse asymmetry with imbalance. Asymmetrical balance is still BALANCE - achieved through unequal but visually weighted elements (e.g., a small dark shape balancing a large light one).

items.q: Which designer is famed for using negative space in mid-twentieth-century film posters?
items.why: It's Saul Bass. “Paul Rand” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which colour model is standard for full-colour print production?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: CMYK. “RGB” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many ink colours does CMYK printing use?
items.why: The real figure is 4 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
